data.title: New Mountain Finance: NAV Continues To Slide Through Q3 (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Overview

Business development companies continue to struggle in this higher interest rate environment. The higher cost of debt essentially limits the appeal for businesses to seek debt financing, which stunts growth for BDCs. New Mountain Finance Corp (NMFC) has faced many of the same challenges as the rest of the sector. When I previously covered NMFC, I issued a hold rating due to the lack of earnings growth and NAV decline. Now that NMFC reported its Q3 earnings, I wanted to revisit the performance and outlook for the remainder of 2025.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that NMFC's share price has declined by about 18.5%. NMFC has essentially failed to participate in the growth of the overall market over the last few months. Even when including all distributions that were paid out to investors, the total return still sits at a loss of 8.6% over the same time frame. NMFC now offers investors a starting dividend yield of 13.4%, which makes it an enticing choice for investors seeking income generation. However, I remain cautious about the overall sustainability of payouts in the near term.

Despite NMFC trading at a large discount to NAV valuation, I believe there is continued downside risk. Management has failed to allocate a meaningful level of capital towards new investment activity, which means that there's no indication that the current NAV decline will shift its momentum. Additionally, NMFC's non-accrual rate has experienced an uptick in non-accruals since my last coverage. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying portfolio strategy and earnings.

Q3 Earnings

According to the latest portfolio breakdown, NMFC's investment portfolio has a fair value of $2.95B that is spread across 127 different portfolio companies. In order to generate its earnings, NMFC lends capital to middle market companies and collects interest and equity in return. NMFC primarily leans on first lien debt securities to generate its earnings, which is ideal because first lien debt sits at the top of the corporate capital structure. This form of debt has the highest priority for repayment, which protects NMFC against losing all invested capital in a bad transaction. The full breakdown by debt type is as follows:

  • First Lien Debt: 67%
  • SLPs: 9%
  • Preferred: 7%
  • Common Equity & Other: 5%
  • Net Lease: 4%
  • Subordinated:4%
  • Second Lien Debt: 4%

Expanding on this diversity, NMFC allocates its portfolio across a diverse range of industries and sub-sectors. The bulk of the portfolio is allocated across Software, Healthcare, and Business Service investments. However, management spreads its investments across a ton of sub-sectors that help mitigate any sort of concentration risk.

NMFC reported its Q3 earnings at the start of November and the results were a bit disappointing. Most notably, net investment income amounted to $0.32 per share, which was in-line with the prior quarters. Total investment income amounted to $80.4M, but this was a decline from the prior quarter's total investment income of $83.4M. The lower income can be attributed to a lesser amount of interest income received during the quarter, which now amounted to $59.4M.

Additionally, NMFC was not able to capture any net realized gains during the quarter. However, the portfolio did see a net change in unrealized depreciation of $21.6M during the quarter. The portfolio position has continued to struggle with appreciating over time, reinforced by twelve straight months of declines.

On a more positive note, NMFC was able to offset these lower earnings by reducing its operating expenses. Management fees saw a slight decline from $9.8M in the prior quarter, to $9.66M in the most recent quarter. Additionally, the incentive fee was nearly cut in half and amounted to $2.8M. However, NMFC did see the amount of interest it's paying on debt rise to $31.7M. Overall, total net expenses declined to $46.5M for the quarter.

From a balance sheet perspective, NMFC has some room for improvement. For instance, the statutory debt to equity ratio has increased to 1.26x, indicating that the portfolio is more reliant on borrowed capital to fuel its growth. Statutory debt for the quarter has increased to $1.58B, up from the prior quarter's total debt of $1.52B. However, NMFC did improve its cash and equivalents, which has now increased to $63.7M.

Valuation & Risk Profile

Since NMFC is structured as a business development company, the price can trade at a difference from the underlying value of its assets. As a result of the continuous decline, NMFC now trades at a large discount to NAV of 20.90%. For reference, NMFC has traded at an average discount to NAV of 5.2% over the last five years. The red line on the chart below clearly indicates that NMFC now trades at one of the largest discounts to NAV levels over the last five years. However, I believe that this discount is a direct representation of the challenges that NMFC continues to face.

Wall Street analysts currently have an average price target of $10 per share, which indicates a small ~4% upside from the current level. While this small upside is certainly possible, I believe the growth potential remains limited. For instance, management was only able to allocate $127.3M towards new investments during the quarter. However, this was offset by repayments totaling $177.1M, which ultimately resulted in a net decline of $49.8M. This is now the fourth consecutive quarter of NMFC experiencing a net decline in new investment activity. This has led to the BDC's NAV trending downward over the last year:

  • Q3 2024: $12.62 per share
  • Q4 2024: $12.55 per share
  • Q1 2025: $12.45 per share
  • Q2 2025: $12.21 per share
  • Q3 2025: $12.06 per share

Additionally, NMFC has seen a slight uptick in its level of non-accruals. Non-accruals represent the amount of portfolio companies that are materially underperforming and cannot keep up with their required debt maintenance. According to the latest earnings call, non-accruals increased to 1.7% of fair portfolio value, indicating that a greater amount of borrowers are struggling to repay their debts. Management confirmed that the uptick can be attributed to weaker consumer demand.

During the quarter, we moved our first lien debt position in Beauty Industry Group to nonaccrual status and expect to equitize a portion of this debt position in the coming months. The company has experienced persistent earnings headwinds due to weaker consumer demand, specific go-to-market challenges and tariffs on its China-oriented supply chain. In coordination with the other lender, we have built a large New Mountain team that will be focused on improving this investment. - John Kline, President

TO NMFC's credit, the non-accrual rate doesn't really sit that much higher than some notable peers in the BDC sector. Despite the Fed initiating its second 25 basis point cut to interest rates, the higher rate environment still limits the amount of businesses seeking debt financing. For reference, here are the non-accrual rates for some peer BDCs:

  • Ares Capital (ARCC): 1% non-accrual rate at fair portfolio value.
  • Barings BDC Inc (BBDC): 0.4% non-accrual rate at fair portfolio value.
  • PennantPark Investment (PNNT): 0.7% non-accrual rate at fair portfolio value.

Dividend Coverage

As of the latest declared quarterly distribution of $0.32 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 13.4%. As previously mentioned, net investment income amounted to $0.32 per share for the quarter, which does indicate that earnings currently support the payouts. However, this leaves essentially no margin for error. So if non-accruals increase and net investment income is negatively impacted, NMFC's distributions will be under threat of reduction.

However, it seems that management is confident in the portfolio's ability to continue supporting payouts. This is achieved through the combination of a dividend protection program and recurring income from their loan portfolio. Unfortunately, management didn't confirm any sort of undistributed spillover income that is available.

Our net investment income and dividend were supported by consistent recurring income from our loan portfolio, full utilization of the dividend protection program, which remains in place through the fourth quarter of 2026 and an incremental fee waiver. Looking forward to Q4, we would like to announce a $0.32 dividend payable on December 31 to shareholders of record on December 17. - Steven Klinsky, CEO

Lastly, investors should be aware that the distributions received from NMFC do not offer any tax efficiency. Since payouts are fueled primarily by net investment income, the distributions will be classified as ordinary dividends, which have the greatest tax burden for investors. So if you want to limit the tax burden from all distributions received, a position in NMFC would be best utilized within a tax-advantaged account.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I am downgrading my rating on NMFC to a sell due to the NAV decline and failure to allocate capital towards new investments during the quarter. Additionally, non-accruals experienced a slight uptick, which is another indication of the challenges that the portfolio faces. If NMFC isn't capable of growing its portfolio through new investments, it is unlikely that the net investment income will grow and the NAV decline will be reversed. So although NMFC can still support its distributions, I believe that there is more downside risk ahead.

data.title: Planet Fitness: Aggressive Growth Targets Through FY 2028
data.description:
Markets are rebounding sharply as we head into year-end, reflecting increased confidence in the prospects for a rate cut and Q3 earnings that largely beat expectations, despite clear signs of weakening consumer spending. It's hardly a time for complacency, however, and with so much of the S&P 500's gains this year being driven by large-cap tech stocks, I think investors should spend effort in the last month of the year ensuring we're not overexposed to the AI trade and rotating toward more "growth at a reasonable price" stocks.

Planet Fitness (PLNT), in my view, is one of these great companies to invest in. Since the start of the year, Planet Fitness has gained ~12%, slightly trailing the S&P 500 despite accelerating growth trends, with enthusiasm driven by price increases in the company's standard membership tier. The company recently reported a healthy beat-and-raise in Q3 that sparked a fresh rally in the name. With a substantial growth opportunity still ahead of it, I think Planet Fitness is still in the earlier stages of a larger rally.

I last wrote a buy article on Planet Fitness in September, when the stock was hovering around $100 per share. Since then, Planet Fitness has initiated new medium-term growth targets that point to an expected acceleration in adjusted EBITDA growth. To me, this is a clear reflection that Planet Fitness is recognizing its pricing power to boost the rates on its still-modestly priced memberships, with little churn recognized to date. As the company is unlocking substantial top- and bottom-line growth, I'm reiterating my buy rating on this company.

Let's first check in on Planet Fitness' valuation multiples against its raised guidance targets before digging into the company's longer-term expectations. At current share prices near $112, Planet Fitness trades at a market cap of $9.33 billion. After we net off the $529.5 million of cash and $2.16 billion of debt on the company's latest balance sheet, Planet Fitness' resulting enterprise value is $10.96 billion.

For the remainder of FY25, Planet Fitness has guided to 11% y/y revenue growth, which is up one point from a previous 10% growth expectation, driven by 50 bps of expected same-club sales growth improvement versus its prior guidance. Management is now also expecting 12% y/y adjusted EBITDA growth (~$546 million in adjusted EBITDA) at a ~41.7% margin.

For FY26, Wall Street is expecting Planet Fitness to generate $1.46 billion in revenue, at an 11% y/y growth pace. If we assume 100 bps of y/y adjusted EBITDA margin expansion to 42.7% margins (flat to Q3 actual margins and roughly in line with the company's current ~1 point of y/y margin expansion), adjusted EBITDA would be $623.4 million (+14% y/y), also in line with the company's plan to grow adjusted EBITDA at a mid-teens pace through the next several years.

This positions Planet Fitness' valuation multiples at:

  • 20.1x EV/FY25 adjusted EBITDA
  • 17.6x EV/FY26 adjusted EBITDA

While Planet Fitness isn't exactly a value stock, I consider it to be a "growth at a reasonable price" company when we consider the substantial growth trajectory that the company is expecting through FY28. In a recent mid-November Investor Day, Planet Fitness laid out new targets, expecting a continuation of mid single digit same-club sales growth (driven by the Black Card price increase in 2026), 6-7% annual franchise unit growth, and mid-teens adjusted EBITDA growth.

To me, Planet Fitness is enjoying two secular tailwinds. The first is a clear trend into health and fitness that is driving gym sign-ups; the second is consumers pinching their budgets, which will accentuate Planet Fitness' value appeal (even after raising prices, Planet Fitness' $15 cost for a standard membership, or $30 for a Black Card with extra perks and all-location access, is still at industry lows). Stay long here and keep riding the recent rally higher.

Q3 recap

Let's now go through Planet Fitness' latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q3 earnings summary is shown below:

Planet Fitness' revenue grew 13% y/y to $330.3 million, beating Wall Street's expectations of $323.4 million (+11% y/y) by a two-point margin. Meanwhile, same club sales growth - which as a reminder, measures the company's revenue growth from existing club locations, excluding the impact of newly opened locations - grew at 6.9% y/y, which is a slight deceleration from 8.2% in Q2, but still higher than 6.1% in Q1.

We appreciate the fact that Planet Fitness' growth is evenly distributed between unit expansion and each club generating more revenue. Within the quarter, Planet Fitness added 35 net-new locations, ending Q3 with 2,795 total Planet Fitness gyms.

Management notes that a strong mix shift up from the Classic to the Black Card and strong membership retention triggered the decision to begin raising Black Card membership prices from $25 to $30 next year. With roughly two-thirds of the company's membership on this tier, the ~20% jump in Black Card rates will drive 13% growth in same-club sales alone, excluding any offsetting churn impacts. Per CEO Colleen Keating's remarks on the Q3 earnings call:

We also saw increased Black Card penetration in the quarter with 66.1% of our total membership now at the higher tier, a 300 basis point increase from the same quarter last year. Consumers continue to recognize the value of the Black Card with the smallest price gap between our 2 membership tiers since we launched the Black Card.

As many of you know, we held off on increasing the price of our Black Card membership until we got on the other side of the Classic Card price increase anniversary. After thoughtful consideration, significant testing and data analysis, we've made the decision to raise the Black Card price to $29.99 after our peak join season in 2026. We're also continuing to test new Black Card amenities such as dry cold plunge and red light technology that would add even more value to our Black Card offering."

Planet Fitness is also encouraging new unit expansion by rolling out a new, smaller club format, which likely has less upfront capex burden. The company notes that 95% of franchisees who were either developing a new location or renovating an existing one chose the new, optimized gym format.

On the profitability side, Planet Fitness continued to benefit from economies of scale, especially as the company cut selling, general, and administrative expenses by -6% y/y to $30.5 million, despite the double-digit growth in revenue and franchise footprint expansion.

As a result, adjusted EBITDA grew 14% y/y to $140.8 million, reflecting a 42.7% margin that improved 60 bps versus 42.1% in the year-ago Q3.

Key takeaways

Planet Fitness is a terrific business with a base of 20.7 million members, a number that is still growing and captive to planned price increases. The company has an aggressive plan to expand its footprint via 6-7% annual locations growth on top of successful recent price increases. Its mid/high teens adjusted EBITDA multiple is a reasonable valuation for a company planning mid-teens adjusted EBITDA growth through the next few years. Stay long here.

data.title: Producer Price Index: Wholesale Inflation Up 0.3% In September
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The latest report on the Producer Price Index (PPI) shows that wholesale inflation for final demand increased by 0.3% in September. This uptick was in line with expectations and follows a -0.1% decline in August. On an annual basis, headline PPI increased 2.7% year-over-year, unchanged from August and consistent with expectations.

Core PPI, which excludes food and energy prices, was up 0.1% month-over-month after a -0.1% decline in August. This was below the expected 0.2% monthly growth. On an annual basis, core PPI cooled to 2.6% in September from 2.9% in August. This was lower than the expected 2.7% reading and is the smallest annual growth since April 2024.

Producer Price Index: Finished Goods

The BLS shifted its focus to the "final demand" PPI series in 2014, but data for these series extend only back to November 2009 for headline PPI and April 2010 for core PPI. Since our analysis emphasizes longer-term trends, we continue to track the legacy PPI for finished goods, which the BLS still includes in its monthly updates. As a later overlay chart will illustrate, the final demand and finished goods indexes remain highly correlated.

In September, the PPI for finished goods rose 1.2% month-over-month, up from 0.1% in August. Year-over-year, headline PPI for finished goods heated up to 3.3% from 2.0% in August. Meanwhile, core PPI for finished goods was up 0.2% on the month, down from 0.4% in August. On an annual basis, core PPI for finished goods was unchanged at 2.9%.

Producer Price Index (PPI) vs. Consumer Price Index (CPI)

Both PPI and CPI illustrate monthly price changes. However, as their names suggest, the Producer Price Index measures price changes from the producer perspective, whereas the Consumer Price Index measures price changes from the consumer perspective.

PPI is thought to be a leading indicator of consumer inflation because, for the most part, when producers pay more for goods and services, they are likely to pass along those higher costs to the consumer. With that being said, during the 2020 recession, producers were unable to pass along price increases, demonstrating the higher volatility of core PPI than core CPI. This relationship is further illustrated in the next chart.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: VT: Sticking With Global Stocks As The Bull Market Matures
data.description:
International stocks are leading the way in 2025. The Vanguard FTSE All-World ex-US ETF (VEU) has returned 29% through Thanksgiving, outpacing the Vanguard Total Stock Market ETF (VTI) by 12 percentage points. Yes, the US Dollar Index (DXY) is down in 2025, but investors should not dismiss what’s happening globally.

Back in April, I had a buy rating on the Vanguard Total World Stock Market ETF (VT). I reiterate a buy rating today. Its valuation remains decent, while the bull market shows few signs of letting up. What’s more, December through early January has tended to be a strong stretch, particularly after big January through October returns.

Foreign Stocks Beating US Stocks YTD, Dollar -8%

According to the issuer, VT invests in both foreign and U.S. stocks, and it seeks to track the performance of the FTSE Global All Cap Index, which covers both well-established and still-developing markets. The ETF has high-growth potential but also high risk and can serve as a solid long-term, low-cost ETF for diversified investors.

VT is a large ETF with $74 billion in assets under management as of November 26, 2025. That’s up from $54 billion at the time of my previous analysis, while its annual expense ratio is low at just 6 basis points. The fund’s trailing 12-month dividend yield is moderate at 1.67%, which is about 0.4 percentage point higher than that of the S&P 500.

Share-price momentum has been very strong ever since the April 9 post-Liberation Day low, earning the product a robust B+ ETF Grade in that category by Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system. In terms of risk, the fund also scores well, thanks to its ample geographic diversification and limited historical realized volatility readings.

On liquidity, VT is highly tradable. Average daily volume is north of 3 million shares, while the median 30-day bid/ask spread is tight at just two basis points, per Vanguard. I like to split up US vs ex-US holdings by account type.

It's not a huge amount of potential tax alpha, but the foreign tax credit can help (particularly for investors with a low marginal tax bracket). The offset is that non-US equity dividends are typically non-qualified (taxed at your income tax rate).

For an update on the portfolio, the 1-star, Gold-rated ETF by Morningstar plots on the top row of the style box, indicating its high bent to global large caps. There’s about 18% access to mid-caps, while small caps make up only 5% of the fund.

As it stands, the price-to-earnings ratio is reasonable at 18.8x. Coupled with a long-term EPS growth rate of 9.3x, and the resulting PEG ratio is 2.0x... not incredibly cheap, but by no means super pricey.

VT: Portfolio & Factor Profiles

As for the sector breakdown, VT is more diverse than the S&P 500. The Information Technology sector commands a material 28% of the fund, though that is about 6 percentage points below the group’s weight in the SPX.

Financials, Industrials, and Consumer Discretionary (three cyclical sectors) are the next largest. As we approach the end of 2025, fully five sectors are less than 5% of the global stock market each.

VT: Holdings & Dividend Information

Seasonally, December has actually tended to feature muted returns. And that lackluster trend has, on average, played out through Q1. So, calendar trends are not wholly bullish when analyzing the past 10 years of price data.

VT: Neutral Seasonality Last 10 Years December-March

The Technical Take

With at least a fair valuation, VT’s technical situation is solid. Notice in the chart below that shares remain in a protracted uptrend. I see support near the $132 to $135 range, while resistance may come into effect at the all-time high of $142.

But with a rising long-term 200-day moving average, the bulls clearly control the primary trend. What’s more, a larger support layer is seen down at $124. Based on the depth of the February to April decline and subsequent breakout later in the second quarter, a measured move upside price target to $147 is in play.

Also take a look at the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph. It printed a series of lower highs while the price ascended. That’s a yellow flag... a bearish RSI divergence to price. Hence, we could see a continuation of the sideways trend. But I expect an ultimate breakout before long.

VT: Current Consolidation Has Support in the Mid-$130s, Rising 200dma

The Bottom Line

I have a buy rating on VT. I see global stocks as fairly priced within a broader bull market. So, stay on board into year-end, with 2026 coming closer into view.

data.title: Hamilton Insurance Group: Positive Momentum Continues
data.description:
Shares of Hamilton Insurance Group (HG) have been an excellent performer over the past year, gaining over 40%. The company has benefitted from a quiet hurricane season, and its unique investment portfolio has performed very well, driving significant gains, allowing investors to grow more comfortable with the approach. I last covered shares of Hamilton in September, when I downgraded shares to a “hold.” In hindsight, I should have simply maintained them as a buy with the stock adding another 12%, though shares did reach my $23.25 buy target before rallying strongly. With updated financials and shares above my price target, now is a good time to revisit HG.

In the company’s third quarter, Hamilton Insurance Group earned $1.32 on a GAAP basis and $1.20 on a run-rate basis. Net premiums were up over 16% from last year to $523 million. As a reminder, Hamilton is a diversified, niche insurance company. Its business is fairly evenly mixed between reinsurance and insurance exposure, with about half occurring in Bermuda and half overseas (much of the reinsurance market is domiciled in Bermuda for tax and regulatory reasons). The company has been aggressively growing and adding scale, with premiums growing about 20% annually over the past five years.

In the third quarter, HG generated $64 million of underwriting income as its combined ratio was a solid 87.8%, an improvement from 93.6% last year. Results were flattered by the fact that Hamilton had no catastrophic losses in the quarter. Typically, Q3 is the seasonal high point for cat losses given the US hurricane season, but no major storms hit the US this year, which is fairly unusual. Last year, catastrophe losses had an 8.5% impact, so this improvement explained the entire improvement in its combined ratio.

Cat losses are unpredictable and can vary significantly by year. This is why I like to look at an insurer’s normalized profitability when doing my valuation analysis, which assumes an average cat load. In some years, like this year, HG will over-earn relative to norms, given a quiet hurricane season, making up for years like 2022 when the hurricane load is particularly bad.

Indeed, current year losses (ex-cat) were actually 2.2% worse than last year at 55.4%. This is still a healthy level, but it is important to emphasize that the improvement in margins was primarily due to the fact that there were no hurricanes, rather than run-rate improvement in underwriting. Excluding cats, underwriting margins face some pressure in my view. As you can see below, insurance premium inflation has been sharply decelerating, rising just 2.2% in September. That may not be sufficient to keep up with claims inflation, causing some underwriting margin tightening for the industry. Margins are currently quite healthy, so the industry can manage some narrowing, though this will still pressure profits, of course.

Its Bermuda segment generated $52 million of underwriting income with a combined ratio of 80.7%. This was dramatically better than the 89.3% last year, given a 13.2% improvement in cat losses. I view 2024’s cat losses as more indicative of a “normal” cat load. The underlying loss ratio increased by 4.6% due to one large loss. Still, there is some pricing pressure building that is likely to limit potential margin expansion and likely tighten margins somewhat.

Reinsurance rates have come under pressure this year, and HG expects that trend to continue with the January 1st renewals. Legislative changes in Florida in particular have reduced losses, which has brought more players into the sector. All else equal, this moderation in rates risks increasing the reinsurance sector’s combined ratios. To the extent, though, that this is being driven by more favorable loss trends, the impact should be manageable.

Its international segment had a combined ratio of 95.4%, leading to $12 million of underwriting income, double last year’s $5 million in profits. Underlying losses were flat, and the entire improvement came from lower catastrophe losses. Net premiums grew 12% to $252 million, driven by growth across essentially all business lines. Management expects growth in E&S to slow as competition is increasing. Combined with weaker pricing overall, that may pressure future profits.

Net investment income was $98 million, led by a strong $54 million contribution from its Two Sigma investment. So far this year, Two Sigma has earned 13%. As I have discussed previously, Hamilton manages its investment portfolio in two sleeves. First, like most insurance companies, HG has a fixed-income portfolio, providing stability and liquidity to make claims payments. It has $3.6 billion in this portfolio. It is very high quality with an average rating of AA-. With a 3.3-year duration, its 4.3% book yield is relatively insulated from declining rates, and I expect book yields to remain around current levels over the next ~12 months. Given its quality, potential credit losses are also likely to be very small.

What sets Hamilton apart is the fact that it has $2.1 billion invested in Two Sigma hedge funds. HG’s investment is segregated from other clients, and 70% is invested in equities with 30% deployed in macro strategies. Since inception, it has returned 13% with a solid 1.41 Sharpe ratio. All investments are in liquid securities, so HG has no exposure to private assets, which have been a source of much scrutiny lately. While there is an opacity to investments in Two Sigma, and the returns will be more volatile than its fixed income portfolio, it has proven to be a sound and accretive investment over multiple years.

Given strong earnings and a solid capital position, during the quarter, Hamilton repurchased $40 million of stock. Thanks to buybacks, its share count is down just over 3% from last year. Alongside earnings, the company also announced a $150 million increase in its share repurchase authorization. There is no timeline associated with this authorization, and HG is under no obligation to repurchase that much.

Back in September, I was targeting $26.25, or about 1.1x book value. Well, book value is now ~$27.00, up about $1.50 sequentially. This does include about $0.85 of intangibles. Book value growth has been sharp this year given favorable investment outcomes and the lack of hurricanes. Particularly with pricing steadily weakening, I view ~1.1x book value as the cap on valuation, or $28.50-$29, depending on the inclusion of intangibles.

That provides another ~5% upside for shares. As such, even with their recent run, I see no rush to sell Hamilton, given its excellent operating results. I do not see enough upside to buy here, but I continue to see HG as a suitable hold, and on pullbacks below $26.25, I would add more shares in this quality insurance company.

data.title: Novo Nordisk: Headwinds On The Surface, Deep Value If You Dig
data.description:
Introduction

Novo Nordisk (NYSE: NVO) is one of those companies that has been on every bargain hunter’s radar as its stock keeps falling on the back of the recent earnings report and drug trial failures. With that in mind as well as my previous coverages, I want to see if Novo Nordisk is still a bargain worth buying.

As a reminder to readers, Novo is a giant in the healthcare sphere, mainly in weight loss/diabetes care. Many experts and analysts expect the company to continue to have a strong grasp on these industries as they become more and more prevalent in the coming decades, with both Ozempic and Wegovy.

Current Dynamics

I’ll first start with the company’s latest earnings release, which was clearly a mixed result. Novo missed on the revenue consensus with total sales of DKK 74.98B ($11.79B), though still a 5.2% Y/Y increase, while Novo actually posted a beat on its EPS, coming in at DKK 4.5 ($0.71). Operating profit saw a massive decline of 30% in Danish kroner due to the restructuring costs as the company is undergoing its transformation. Though the biggest reaction came from the newest iteration of the growth forecast for the year, which fell to 11%, and operating profit went back down to 7% from the last iteration that came in at 14% and 10%. This was all due to the lower growth expectations for GLP-1 treatments and was a stark difference from NVO’s closest competitor, Eli Lilly (LLY), which actually raised its outlook for 2025 thanks to solid demand for its weight-loss portfolio.

And while this is not all positive, I was impressed when looking deeper at each segment to see the company's obesity care growing by 41% over the 9 months CER with international operations seeing an 83% jump, while US operations grew by 24%. This segment mainly encompasses Wegovy and Saxenda, while on the Ozempic side, it still remains the core driver for the company, though it has posted slower but still steady growth. Mainly, Rybelsus is gaining traction in markets such as China. Rare disease also posted a relatively strong growth at 13%, showing good diversification outside of the metabolic core.

Following the earnings report, Novo as well as LLY announced that they entered into a major agreement with the US administration in terms of drug pricing. For Novo, this will cover all GLP-1 drugs to lower prices but expand coverage as well as manufacturing commitments. For example, the price will drop to $245 per month for Medicare & Medicaid while initially being listed at over $1000. Moreover, the TrumpRx price should come in at $350 per month. Though the net positive for Novo would be the inclusion into Medicare Part D, which was initially prohibited from covering anti-obesity medications. The pilot program will start initially in mid-2026, potentially improving sales. And the kicker to all of this would be that with the manufacturing commitments in the US, Novo should receive a three-year tariff exemption on imported branded drugs. Though Novo believes that this will be a negative, low single-digit impact on global sales growth in 2026, this has been perceived as a better-than-feared outcome, as the expanded coverage will be able to offset the lower prices. And I have the same opinion, as it pushes Novo in the good favor of the current administration.

More recently, Novo’s stock cratered by over 10%, though it recovered during the day, as the company released the disappointing trial results regarding oral semaglutide and Alzheimer’s disease. The main goal of this was to demonstrate a significant reduction in the progression of the disease over a two-year period. However, Rybelsus failed to meet the primary endpoint in the two trials performed, which directly translates to no clinical efficacy. I believe that the news was overblown, as the possibilities were already facing slim chances, and many analysts were only estimating a slim success rate. The stock has recovered since, as the market catches on to the low probabilities. It is slightly disappointing considering that it is again a slight setback for the company, as it captured the imagination of investors about the new market avenues for GLP-1s, yet it does not really change anything for the company, and this highlights the key need for the company to focus all resources towards its growth engines, Ozempic and Wegovy.

I also want to touch on the battle for Metsera, which pushed Novo against Pfizer in order to scoop Metsera, a US-based developer of next-generation GLP-1 therapies. As a shareholder, I’m happy that Novo withdrew from the bid, as a lengthy legal battle with Pfizer right now is unwarranted, and I’d much rather see Novo fix its house before looking at expensive M&A, even if I believe that Metsera has great potential. But this tells us that Novo is currently concerned about retaking market share by buying up the potential next blockbuster therapies in the industry.

Valuation

Novo is still, in my opinion, the greatest bargain in the healthcare industry, especially considering the continuous drop in its share price. Currently, its P/E is the lowest among its peers at 12.66, even below companies like Pfizer and Merck that have also faced headwinds. And though yes, as we have seen with the current dynamics, there are certainly reasons for it, yet I believe that it is overblown, especially as we compare Novo to Eli Lilly. Lilly is facing the same issues in terms of price cuts, yet it is the first pharmaceutical company to reach over a $1 trillion market capitalization, in part thanks to the same deal with the US government. Furthermore, thanks to the cost measures, Novo is currently improving its FCF generation and is having one of the strongest FCF yields of the past decade at 4.3%.

And this depreciated valuation is going on while the company is still having relatively strong growth. Despite a slowing guidance for the year, Novo still grew its revenue by 23.59% CAGR and net income by 25.24% CAGR over the past 3 years, with the revenue figure being the highest among its peers. Looking ahead, Novo needs even more to have great execution on the upcoming approval and launch of its oral semaglutide in the US as to catch up to Eli Lilly and regain its valuation premium. Furthermore, I still expect the GLP-1 market to grow easily over the end of the 2020s, which will leave room for both Eli Lilly and Novo Nordisk to substantially grow their own side of the market.

Despite all of the negatives, Novo is still seeing some of the strongest margins in the pharmaceutical industry, with its gross margin currently at 83.4% and net margin at 32.9%. That’s higher than Eli Lilly, while Novo has a P/E standing at a fourth of Lilly’s. The restructuring costs will, in the short term, drive down operating profit, yet I still believe that this is a net positive in the future and should improve overall margins as the company got too bloated post-COVID. I still want to see how the aggressive pricing strategies will change the company’s profitability, yet I believe that Novo will be able to offset those downward pressures. Furthermore, I expect that Novo will keep or slightly improve its return metrics that are already some of the best on the market, with an ROE of 71.5% and an ROCE of over 65%, which is simply impressive.

Risks

At the moment, the main risk for Novo, in my opinion, would be competition, even if I believe that the space could grow at such a pace that everyone’s a winner. Lilly has simply been the better company over the past couple of years, taking away from the initial thunder that Novo had, and right now the situation has evolved towards who can have the most effective weight-loss pill, which could be a way for NVO to regain its premium. There are some other up-and-coming rivals, albeit they are lacking the manufacturing scale and are years away from commercialization.

Novo is also more and more concentrated on a single compound, and even if it is highly successful, it creates a risk compared to other pharmaceutical companies like Pfizer and Roche. Any sort of issue, such as safety or patent challenges, could directly put in question the largest portion of the company’s revenue.

Conclusion

Despite all of the recent negatives on the surface, I still believe that Novo is sound and solid with its position in the semaglutide market and its current valuation. It is riskier than investing in a company like Pfizer, but it is certainly rare to stumble into a 12x P/E company that is posting such growth rates. Outside of common shares, I currently own LEAPS expiring in September and December at a strike price of $60, and I will certainly look to add to the position. I keep my Strong Buy rating on Novo Nordisk.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: UGL: Amplify Exposure To The Dollar Debasement Trade
data.description:
The ProShares Ultra Gold ETF (UGL) is a leveraged strategy designed to provide traders with 2x the daily performance of the Bloomberg Gold Subindex. Thematically, gold has rallied throughout 2025 as the dollar debasement trade intended to protect investors’ wealth from deterioration of the US dollar. Though UGL doesn’t provide direct exposure to gold, it will allow investors to participate in the potential volatility as the spot price for gold hovers above $4,000/oz.

About ProShares Ultra Gold ETF

UGL was launched by ProShares on December 1, 2008 on the NYSE Arca Exchange. The strategy has a net expense ratio of 95bps, on par with peer leveraged strategies. UGL has roughly $900mm in net assets with an average of $153.75mm in share value changing hands on a daily basis, providing investors and traders with substantial liquidity for actively managing a position.

UGL has 200% exposure to gold, investing in commodity futures and index swaps. These derivative contracts allow for the fund to gear up the portfolio to provide traders with 2x the daily performance of the underlying asset. Futures and swap positions offer relatively similar exposure, though differ to a certain degree.

Gold futures will provide direct exposure to the underlying commodity in reference to the spot and futures price, converging near the end of the contract date. Futures contracts are settled daily after market close. Swap contracts provide exposure to the daily performance of the Bloomberg Gold Subindex in exchange for a fixed rate fee. Swap contracts are settled daily after market close, exchanging cash earned from performance for the fixed rate. Each derivative exhibits a daily reset feature where performance is measured on a day-to-day basis rather than a rolling basis.

The daily reset is reflected in UGL in which the ETF, which may result in a compounding effect if held for longer than a single trading day. Though compounded returns can be beneficial to the upside as investors may realize gains beyond the 2x daily performance target, the same phenomenon works to the downside in which losses can cascade fast and potentially result in unsalvageable losses.

Why Trade Gold?

Gold has been making headlines lately with growing interest in the dollar debasement trade. This trading strategy essentially offsets US dollar currency risk by investing in alternative assets like gold and bitcoin. The theory behind the trade is that the US dollar is deteriorating as a result of the mounting national debt and heightened inflationary pressures. Essentially, the dollar debasement trade is aimed at protecting wealth rather than growing it.

Theoretically, assuming gold and bitcoin is priced in US dollars, the value of gold and bitcoin can increase relative to the US dollar if the value of the US dollar were to decrease, meaning that the value of gold and bitcoin may remain stagnant while the relative price appreciates with respect to the US dollar. This plays into the inflation theory that commodities and goods are becoming more expensive with respect to the US dollar but may remain fairly priced with respect to gold or bitcoin. Since we don’t buy goods with bitcoin or gold, testing the dollar debasement trade will be more of a theoretical exercise rather than for utilization.

The challenge with arguing the devaluation of the US dollar is the relative measurement of the decline in value. With respect to foreign currencies, the US dollar has remained relatively strong, reflected in the US dollar index.

In terms of inflation, the US dollar can be measured as weakening with respect to the basket of goods inflation is measured against.

The challenge in comparing these factors to the value of gold and bitcoin is that goods measured in the Consumer Price Index aren’t necessarily purchased with gold or bitcoin, providing investors with limited insights into the relative value of these alternative currencies with respect to goods and services.

Nonetheless, the driver with respect to UGL is that gold has gained significant interest amongst traders and may provide more volatility given the significant run-up in the price of gold throughout 2025. An indicator that may provide insights into the expected volatility of gold is fund flows. Using the SPDR Gold Shares ETF (GLD) as a proxy for physical gold, we can observe growing interest in physical gold going into the end of October 2025 before turning to net selling.

Though I don’t believe a month of activity will determine the direction of gold going forward, I believe the fund flows may indicate peak euphoria for the trade that has already occurred and that the price may stabilize or be set for a pullback following the significant run-up throughout 2025. This may create more volatility in the price of gold, providing traders with an opportunity to actively trade the price swings using UGL to amplify exposure.

Risks related to UGL

UGL is a leveraged strategy designed to provide traders with 2x the daily performance of gold, exposing traders and investors to certain risks that must be considered prior to making a final investment decision. Leveraged strategies are meant to be held for a single trading day; holding UGL beyond a single day may result in compounded returns that may result in unsalvageable losses if a trade goes south. Leveraged strategies may also exhibit value decay over time that can impact ETF performance. Given the heightened risks of trading leveraged funds, I believe traders must remain disciplined in their trading and risk management strategies and know when to cut losses.

Liquidity and volatility may impact the pricing of the fund and may result in UGL not achieving its 2x daily performance target. Traders must take into consideration fees and taxes when trading UGL; traders may be exposed to higher short-term capital gains that may impact the total returns of their trading strategies.

You can review additional risks here and the SEC bulletin here.

Final Thoughts

UGL can provide traders with amplified returns with respect to the price of gold, benefiting from increased public interest in the dollar debasement trade. The price of gold may become more volatile following the run-up in value in the last year, potentially creating more opportunities for active traders to profit on swing trades. Given the risks involved in holding leveraged strategies, investors must use caution if seeking to hold UGL for longer than a single trading day as gains and losses may compound over time. Given the risks involved in owning leveraged strategies, I am recommending UGL with a Hold rating.

data.title: U.S. IPO Weekly Recap: IPO Market Quiet In Short Thanksgiving Week
data.description:
Two blank check companies debuted this week, while two IPOs and three SPACs submitted initial filings. Also on the SPAC side, Soulpower Acquisition (SOUL) announced it would merge with SWB Holdings at a proposed $8.1 billion valuation, one of the largest contemplated combinations announced this year.

Two SPACs priced this week: Invest Green Acquisition (IGACU) raised $150 million to target renewable energy, while Nukkleus-backed SC II Acquisition (SCIIU) also raised $150 million.

2 IPOs During the Week of November 24th, 2025
Issuer Business Deal Size Market Cap at IPO Price vs. Midpoint First Day Return Return at 11/28

SC II Acquisition (SCIIU)

 $150M $217M 0% +0% +0%
Blank check company formed by Nukkleus and led by the company's CEO.

Invest Green Acquisition (IGACU)

 $150M $208M 0% +0% +0%
Blank check company targeting renewable energy, sustainable finance, and nuclear energy.

Oncology biotech Edison Oncology Holding (EOHC) filed to raise $25 million at a $75 million market cap. IoT hardware and software provider Direct Communication Solutions (DCSX) filed to raise $15 million.

Three SPACs submitted initial filings this week. Mountain Lake Acquisition II (MLAAU), the second blank check company led by the chairman of Axos Financial, filed to raise $261 million. Vine Hill Capital II (VHCPU) filed to raise $175 million to target industrials, transportation, and technology. Social Commerce Partners (SCPQU) filed to raise $100 million to target the direct selling industry.

5 Filings During the Week of November 24th, 2025
Issuer Business Deal Size Sector Lead Underwriter

DCS (OTCPK:DCSX)

 $15M Technology Revere Sec.
Provides IoT hardware, software, and connectivity solutions.

Edison Oncology (EOHC)

 $25M Health Care Konik Capital Partners
Phase 2 biotech developing targeted small-molecule oncology therapies.

Mountain Lake Acq. II (MLAAU)

 $261M SPAC BTIG
Second blank check company led by the chairman of Axos Financial.

Vine Hill Capital II (VHCPU)

 $175M SPAC Stifel
Blank check company targeting industrials, transportation, and technology.

Social Commerce Partners (SCPQU)

 $100M SPAC BTIG
Blank check company targeting the direct selling industry.

Week Ahead

No IPOs are currently scheduled to price in the week ahead.

We expect the 2025 IPO calendar to start December on a relatively quiet note, although a handful of issuers may still look to price before year-end. Medical supplies giant Medline (MDLN) remains the potential headline event and could raise an estimated $5 billion in an offering that would mark the largest US IPO since 2021.

Several other sizable deals are also eligible to launch in December. Pan-Asian travel platform Klook (KLK), crypto investment platform Grayscale (GRAY), and satellite manufacturer York Space Systems (YSS) all submitted initial filings in recent weeks, and could list in the coming weeks. Meanwhile, offshore transport service provider Hornbeck Offshore Services (HOS), construction company Cardinal Infrastructure (CDNL), and tax advisory firm Andersen Group (ANDG) all recently updated their prospectuses with new financials and could also go before year-end.

Street research is expected for six companies, and three lock-up periods will be expiring in the week ahead.

IPO Market Snapshot

The Renaissance IPO Indices are market cap-weighted baskets of newly public companies. As of 11/26/2025, the Renaissance IPO Index was up 4.7% year-to-date, while the S&P was up 17.2%. Renaissance Capital’s IPO ETF (IPO) tracks the index, and top ETF holdings include Kenvue (KVUE) and Reddit (RDDT). The Renaissance International IPO Index was up 32.2% year-to-date, while the ACWX was up 28.7%. Renaissance Capital’s International IPO ETF (IPOS) tracks the index, and top ETF holdings include Galderma and Horizon Robotics.

Original Post

data.title: UGL: A $4,200 Gold Breakout Play
data.description:
After breaking out to a new all-time high near $4,400 in October, Gold (XAUUSD:CUR) has spent the last several weeks consolidating between $3,900 and $4,100 per ounce. With Gold once again moving above $4,200 as November winds down to a close, traders and investors may be looking for ways to capitalize on further price gains in the yellow metal through the end of the year. In this article, I'll initiate coverage of the ProShares Ultra Gold ETF (UGL) and explain why I like the product over other 'leveraged Gold' alternatives.

ProShares Ultra Gold ETF

I've covered leveraged funds for Seeking Alpha numerous times over the last several years. To briefly summarize my view on leveraged products like UGL, I believe they are tremendous for traders and short-term holding but not ideal for long-term investment. Given this, I rarely call these types of funds a 'buy' unless I have a high degree of conviction in a multi-month bullish trend - as was the case when I recently covered the ProShares Ultra Silver ETF (AGQ) earlier in November. That fund is up over 14% in roughly two weeks, and I suspect that move is just getting started.

Standard spot funds like the SPDR Gold Shares ETF (GLD) allocate to physical metal. As a leveraged fund, UGL primarily utilizes futures contracts in an attempt to produce a 2x return over Gold's daily performance:

Fund Comparison UGL GLD
Fund Type Miscellaneous Commodities
Issuer ProShares SPDR State Street Investment Management
Inception 12/01/2008 11/18/2004
Expense Ratio 0.95% 0.40%
AUM $892.14M $139.10B

Source: Seeking Alpha

Due to active management and contract rollover, UGL has an admittedly larger expense ratio compared with GLD at 0.95%. Both funds have roughly two decades in the market, yet GLD is far larger by AUM at over $139 billion compared with just $892 million for UGL. Since UGL is leveraged and regularly rebalanced, the fund is not something long-term investors want to hold unless Gold's directional trend is clear. Consider the performance of UGL versus GLD between July 2020 and July 2023:

During this three year period, the price of Gold didn't go much of anywhere, though it did produce a modest gain. UGL holders would have lost money over that same period of time. Because it is leveraged, UGL will amplify losses when Gold goes down, maximize gains when Gold goes up, and decay during times of sideways action. Thus, it is critical to time entries well when looking at UGL for a Gold trade.

Gold's Technical Breakout

A breakout like the one we just got in Gold is where I think it makes sense to go long UGL. Those who have followed my prior SA work may remember where I laid out what I believed were Gold's 'buy zone' and 'load the boat zone' back in late October. While we were close, we never did quite get into the 'buy zone' below $3,800.

However, what we do have is an asset that has reclaimed its 20-day moving average as it takes out its prior closing high from mid-November. You can also see in the chart above that I've drawn trend-line resistance from the October peak down to the mid-November relief rally peak. Gold has soundly taken out that trend as of article submission. Barring some sort of catastrophic reversal in the metal, my view is this is a clean breakout in a secular bull trend that shouldn't be faded.

UGL versus Leveraged Alternatives

It's important to reiterate that leveraged funds, even funds that leverage from the long side, are inherently riskier than basic un-leveraged funds or equity market alternatives like mining stocks. However, the reason why I personally like using products like UGL following breakouts is because I view Gold equities as risky for different reasons. For instance, if I believe the price of Gold is going higher (as I do) and I want outperformance over a 1:1 spot ETF or closed-end fund, I have a reasonable degree of certainty that UGL will give me the 2:1 return that I want. That is not necessarily the case with the miners for a variety of reasons:

  1. Public miner equity is often at risk of dilution
  2. Mining stocks have management performance risk
  3. Mining stocks have jurisdiction risk

It's not as big of an issue with the larger cap mining stocks, but the further down the cap rankings you go, the bigger the dilution risk is with Gold mining stocks. Especially when the companies down cap are either in discovery or growing out production. As with any equity, investors are betting on the horses just as much as they're betting on the theme or idea; this is especially true with mining stocks, where expertise in the field is essential. Finally, mining stocks also have varying jurisdiction risks to consider. Depending on where a mine is located in the world, there can certainly be mine nationalization considerations. While that may not necessarily be an overt risk today, in a world where Gold becomes a more appreciated form of monetary/fiscal collateral, Gold miners face increasing nationalization risk, in my view.

Then there's also the proof of the tape itself. If we go back to when Gold broke out from its decade-long ceiling of $1,900-2,000 back in November 2023, UGL has simply outperformed the mining sector on a total return basis:

Not only has UGL outperformed the VanEck Gold Miners ETF (GDX) over the last two years, but the fund has indeed outperformed its 2x goal against simply holding Gold. This is not to say that investors should avoid miners entirely in favor of a leveraged fund like UGL. To be sure, I believe there are tremendous opportunities available in the market in the mining space, and I own several individual names as well. For those who want mining exposure, I highly recommend Taylor Dart's research service, having previously subscribed.

Closing Summary

After more than a month of consolidating around $4,000, Gold seems poised to continue its secular rally to new highs based on strong fundamentals and a technical breakout over trend resistance. While I certainly wouldn't recommend having a larger allocation of investment capital to a product like UGL over something like the Sprott Physical Gold Trust (PHYS), I think UGL can be a highly useful product for traders or investors who want a little juice over what Gold will provide all on its own. There is a time and a place for mining stocks or mining funds. I own those as well. But for straightforward gold exposure in a secular bull, it's tough to argue against UGL for those who actively trade or self-direct investments with shorter-term time horizons. I'm initiating UGL with a 'buy.' I personally hold shares in the fund and expect to hold them through at least the end of 2025. In the event that changes, I will provide readers with an update.
